elements.sent-2411-001: The interval count problem, a classical question in the study of interval orders, was introduced by Ronald Graham in the 1980s.
elements.sent-2411-002: This problem asks: given an interval order $P$, what is the minimum number of distinct interval lengths required to construct an interval representation of $P$? Interval orders that can be represented with just one interval length are known as semiorders, and their characterizations are well known.
elements.sent-2411-003: However, the characterization of interval orders that require at most $k$ interval lengths — termed $k$-count interval orders—remains an open and challenging problem for $k\geq 2$.
elements.sent-2411-004: Our investigation into $2$-count interval orders led us naturally to consider a related problem, interval representations of permutations, which we introduce in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-005: Specifically, we characterize permutations that have a $2$-count interval representation.
elements.sent-2411-006: We prove that a permutation admits a $2$-count interval representation if and only if its longest decreasing subsequences have length at most $2$.
elements.sent-2411-007: For larger values of $k$, however, a similar characterization does not hold.
elements.sent-2411-008: There are permutations that do not permit a $3$-count interval representation despite having decreasing subsequences of length at most $3$.
elements.sent-2411-010: The $k$-count permutation representation problem appears to capture essential aspects of the broader problem of characterizing $k$-count interval orders.
elements.sent-2411-011: To support this connection, we apply our findings on interval representations of permutations to demonstrate that a height-$3$ interval order is $2$-count if and only if it has depth at most $2$, where the depth of an interval order refers to the length of the longest nested chain of intervals required in any interval representation of the order.
elements.sent-2411-012: A finite poset $P=(X, \prec)$ is an interval order if it has an interval representation, a family of compact intervals $\{I_x : x \in X\}$ in $\mathbb{R}$ such that $x \prec y$ if and only if $I_x$ is entirely to the left of $I_y$, for all $x, y \in X$.
elements.sent-2411-013: An interval representation of $P$ using $k$ distinct interval lengths is called a {\it $k$-count representation of $P$.}
elements.sent-2411-014: The {\it depth of an interval order is the maximum number of intervals in a chain of nested intervals need to occur in every representation of the interval order.}
elements.sent-2411-015: A systematic study of $k$-count representations for interval graphs and interval orders was initiated by Fishburn \cite{FishburnBook a half a century ago.}
elements.sent-2411-016: Fishburn proved \cite[Section 9, Theorem 1]{FishburnBook that for arbitrary $k$, there are depth-2 interval orders with no $k$-count interval representation.}
elements.sent-2411-017: He found the smallest $2$-count interval order with no $2$-count representation that has $10$ elements \cite[Section 9, Example 2]{FishburnBook .}
elements.sent-2411-018: The characterization of interval orders having a $k$-count representation and the complexity status of the corresponding recognition problems are still open for $k\geq 2$ in general.
elements.sent-2411-019: There are results for $k=2$ with additional restrictions \cite{BoyadzhiyskaDissertation,BGT,FMOS,JLORS .}
elements.sent-2411-020: Here we discuss another particular case for $k=2$.
elements.sent-2411-021: Our main result is Theorem \ref{2count , where we give a characterization of height-3 posets having a 2-count interval representation.}
elements.sent-2411-022: This theorem supports our conjecture stated in Section \ref{main on the forbidden structure characterization of a {$\mathbf{4 +\mathbf{1}$}-free interval order to be 2-count ({$\mathbf{4 +\mathbf{1}$} is a 5-element poset, the union of a chain on four elements and a distinct incomparable element).}}}
elements.sent-2411-023: The heart of the proof of the main theorem is an application of Theorem \ref{2countperm on the $2$-count interval representations of permutations.}
elements.sent-2411-024: Section \ref{prelim introduces the $k$-count problems for permutations.}
elements.sent-2411-025: The proof of Theorem \ref{2countperm , a procedure that solves a corresponding linear system of inequalities, is given in Section \ref{mutations .}}
elements.sent-2411-026: By the classical theorem of Scott and Suppes \cite{ScottSuppes , an interval order has 1-count interval representation if and only if it contains no {$\mathbf{3 +\mathbf{1}$} subposet; equivalently if it is a semiorder ({$\mathbf{3 +\mathbf{1}$} is a 4-element poset, the union of a chain on three elements and a distinct incomparable element).}}}
elements.sent-2411-027: A necessary condition for the existence of a $2$-count representation is that the interval order has depth at most two, that is, there is no nested chain of more than two intervals, which occurs in every representation of the interval order (see Proposition \ref{nested ).}
elements.sent-2411-028: Notice that forbidding a {$\mathbf{4 +\mathbf{1}$} subposet from a depth-2 interval order is still not sufficient to guarantee a $2$-count interval representation.}
elements.sent-2411-029: For convenience, we identify an interval order with its ascent sequence (see Bousquet-Mélou et al. \cite{ascent2010 ).}
elements.sent-2411-030: So, to introduce the interval order $P$ corresponding to the ascent sequence $(0,1,2,0,3,2,3,0,2,4,2)$ we write $P=(0,1,2,0,3,2,3,0,2,4,2)$.
elements.sent-2411-031: The Hasse diagram and canonical representation of this interval order are presented in Figure \ref{Andre11 .}
elements.sent-2411-032: Computer search showed that $P=(0,1,2,0,3,2,3,0,2,4,2)$ is minimal in the sense that no depth-2, {$\mathbf{4 +\mathbf{1}$}-free interval order exists with less than 11 elements that has no 2-count representation.}
elements.sent-2411-033: The magnitude $m$ of an interval order $P$ is defined as the minimum number of endpoints of an interval representation of $P$.
elements.sent-2411-034: Greenough \cite{Greenough proved that the minimal endpoint representation is essentially unique.}
elements.sent-2411-035: The minimal endpoint representation of $P$ with endpoints $0,1,\ldots,m-1$ is called the {\it canonical representation of $P$.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Greenough \cite{Greenough also proved that in the canonical representation every point is the left endpoint and the right endpoint of some interval.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In particular, in a canonical representation the singleton intervals $[0,0]$ and $[m-1,m-1]$ are present.
elements.sent-2411-038: By the classical theorem of Scott and Suppes \cite{ScottSuppes , an interval order has 1-count interval representation if and only if it contains no {\bf 3+1 subposet; equivalently if it is a semiorder.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: Each copy of a {\bf 3+1 subposet in $P$ determines a {\it peel and {\it pith pair in the representation of $P$, that is, the singleton element $x$ and the middle element $y$ of the 3-chain, respectively (see Figure \ref{ppair ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: In our diagrams a peel colored red is longer than a pith colored green.
elements.sent-2411-041: Peel-pith pairs in an interval order $P=(X,\prec)$ can be presented in form of an auxiliary graph.
elements.sent-2411-042: Denote PP the digraph on vertex set $X$ and an arrow from the pith to the peel occurring in a copy of {\bf 3+1 subposet in $P$ , for each peel-pith pair.}
elements.sent-2411-043: In the next proposition we state the obvious fact that the inclusion of peel-pith pairs is forced in every representation of an interval order.
elements.sent-2411-044: An antichain $A=\{a_1,\ldots,a_t\}$ of an interval order $P$ forms a nested chain of intervals in every representation of $P$ if and only if $A$ induces a directed path in the {\em PP -graph of $P$.}
elements.sent-2411-045: The number of elements of a longest directed path in graph PP is the {\it depth of the interval order.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The {\it height of an interval order is the number of elements in a longest chain of the poset.}
elements.sent-2411-047: A semiorder has depth one since it is $\mathbf{3 +\mathbf{1}$-free, thus its PP has no arc.}
elements.sent-2411-048: On the other hand, semiorders can have arbitrary height since a chain is a semiorder.
elements.sent-2411-049: Here we investigate 2-count representations of an interval order $P$.
elements.sent-2411-050: We assume that $P$ has depth 2; this is an obvious necessary condition for the existence of a 2-count representation.
elements.sent-2411-051: A 2-count representation produces a $2$ coloring that is a partition $T\cup S$ of the elements of $P$, where $T$ is the set of elements represented with long intervals, and $S$ is the set of elements represented with short intervals.
elements.sent-2411-052: In the diagrams long intervals and elements of $T$ are colored red, short intervals and elements of $S$ are colored green.
elements.sent-2411-053: A red/green coloring of an interval order $P$ is {\it feasible if it comes from a 2-count representation of $P$.}
elements.sent-2411-054: Besides peel-pith pairs, there are further constraints to classifying intervals to be short or long in every representation.
elements.sent-2411-055: We introduce a family of posets embodying such constraints.
elements.sent-2411-056: A {\it spring pattern\footnote{\ The name comes from a physical metaphor identifying interval length constraints as acting forces.}}
elements.sent-2411-057: Notice that $5,3$ form a peel-pith pair.
elements.sent-2411-058: The family of springs consists of all partially colored posets that are defined by Figure \ref{Sp1 and the dual of these posets.}

elements.sent-2411-066: While the exclusion of {$\mathbf{3 +\mathbf{1}$} subposets completely characterizes 1-count interval orders, forbidding {$\mathbf{4 +\mathbf{1}$} subposets from interval orders is not consequential regarding the existence of a 2-count interval representation.}}

elements.sent-2411-068: A {$\mathbf{4 +\mathbf{1}$}-free, depth-2 interval order $\Po$ admits a 2-count representation if and only if $P$ has a $2$-coloring such that every peel is red, every pith is green, and no spring pattern occurs as a colored subposet.}

elements.sent-2411-070: In fact, in this case, neither a copy of {$\mathbf{4 +\mathbf{1}$} nor a spring appear as a subposet of $\Po$, since each has height 4.}
elements.sent-2411-071: An {\it interval representation of a permutation $\pi$ of $[n]$ is a family of real intervals $ [\ell_j,r_j]_{i=1 ^n$ with integral endpoints satisfying \begin{equation}\label{endpoints} \ell_{\pi(1)}<\cdots< \ell_{\pi(n)}<r_1< \cdots<r_n,\end{equation} that is, the right endpoints are in natural order, and the left endpoints correspond to the permutation (see an example in Figure \ref{abra1 ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-072: A permutation $\pi$ is {\it $k$-count if $\pi$ has an interval representation such that the number of distinct interval lengths is $k$.}
elements.sent-2411-073: The {\it depth of $\pi$ is defined as the length of the longest decreasing subsequence of $\pi$, that is a longest chain of nested intervals in an interval representation of $\pi$; if this number is $k$, then $\pi$ is called a {\it depth-k permutation .}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The depth and interval count numbers have the obvious relation that any interval representation of a depth-$k$ permutation uses at least $k$ distinct lengths in a nested chain of intervals.
elements.sent-2411-075: We prove in Section \ref{mutations that {a permutation has a 2-count interval representation if and only if it has depth at most $2$} (Theorem \ref{2countperm ).}}
elements.sent-2411-076: Note that equality between depth and interval count numbers similar to Theorem \ref{2countperm does not hold for depth-$k$ permutations, $k\geq 3$.}
elements.sent-2411-077: There are several examples of depth-3 permutations admitting no 3-count integral representation.
elements.sent-2411-078: Computer search shows that $\pi=[4,2,5,10,3,1,7,6,9,8]$ is a smallest depth-3 permutation with no 3-count representation.
elements.sent-2411-079: An interval representation of this permutation is displayed in Figure \ref{abra6 .}
elements.sent-2411-081: We include a verification at the end of the paper.
elements.sent-2411-082: An indexed partition $(T_1,\ldots,T_k)$ of $[n]$ is called a sorted $k$-coloring, if for all inversions $(x,y)$ such that $x\in T_i$ and $y\in T_j$, we have $i<j$.
elements.sent-2411-083: In terms of an interval representation $\{I_j\}_{j\in[n]}$ of a permutation, $I_x\subsetneq I_y$ implies that $x\in T_i$ and $y\in T_j$ with $i<j$.
elements.sent-2411-084: If the depth of a permutation $\pi$ is $k$, then $\pi$ has a sorted $k$-coloring.
elements.sent-2411-085: Consider the inclusion order $P$ of the intervals in any interval representation of $\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-086: Notice that the chains of $P$ correspond to the decreasing subsequences of $\pi$, so the height of $P$ is $k$.
elements.sent-2411-087: By successive removal of minimal elements, $P$ can be partitioned into antichains $T_1,\ldots,T_k$ (c.f.\ Mirsky's Theorem \cite{Mirsky ).}
elements.sent-2411-088: It is immediate from the definition of $P$ that these form a sorted $k$-coloring.
elements.sent-2411-089: A sorted $k$-coloring $T_1,\ldots,T_k$ of $\pi$ is {\it feasible if there are integers $\alpha_1<\cdots <\alpha_k$ and an interval representation $\{I_j\}_{j\in[n]}$ of $\pi$ such that $|I_x|=\alpha_i$ for every $x\in T_i$.}

elements.sent-2411-170: Here we discuss another particular case for $k=2$; in Theorem \ref{2count we prove a characterization of height-3 posets having a 2-count interval representation.}
elements.sent-2411-171: While the exclusion of {$\mathbf{3 +\mathbf{1}$} subposets from interval orders completely characterizes semiorders (1-count interval orders), forbidding {$\mathbf{4 +\mathbf{1}$} subposets from interval orders is not decisive regarding the existence of a 2-count interval representation.}}
elements.sent-2411-172: However, we conjecture that excluding springs and {$\mathbf{4 +\mathbf{1}$} produces $2$-count interval orders.}
elements.sent-2411-173: Here we prove a special case of Conjecture \ref{4plus1 , where the height of $\Po$ is 3.}
elements.sent-2411-174: In fact, in this case neither {$\mathbf{4 +\mathbf{1}$} nor Springs are subposets of $\Po$, since each has height 4.}
elements.sent-2411-175: A height-3 interval order has a 2-count interval representation if and only if it has depth at most $2$.
elements.sent-2411-176: We prove that the condition of $P$ having depth 2 is sufficient; necessity is obvious.
elements.sent-2411-177: The proof is a procedure that starts with the canonical representation of $P$, then this representation is modified to obtain one with two different interval lengths in three main stages.
elements.sent-2411-178: Let $\mathcal{C}$ be the canonical representation of $P$.
elements.sent-2411-179: In a diagram canonical intervals are positioned between vertical {\it lines labeled $0,1,2,\ldots,m-1$, where $m$ is the magnitude of $P$ representing the endpoints.}
elements.sent-2411-180: In $\mathcal{C}$ each line contains several coinciding endpoints.
elements.sent-2411-181: The first stage of the procedure consists of `breaking ties' leading to another representation of $P$, where there are no coinciding interval endpoints of the same type.
elements.sent-2411-182: It turns out that the bulk of this new representation of $P$ consists of intervals forming an interval representation of some permutation of depth-2.
elements.sent-2411-183: In the second stage of the procedure, we apply Theorem \ref{2countperm leading to a 2-count representation of the `middle part' with interval lengths $\alpha<\beta$, where $\alpha$ is sufficiently large.}
elements.sent-2411-184: A canonical interval with an endpoint at $0$ or $m-1$ is called {\it extremal .}
elements.sent-2411-185: Note that every line in a canonical representation contains a left endpoint and a right endpoint, thus $\mathcal{C}$ contains $[0,0]$, $[m-1,m-1]$ (see \cite{Greenough [Corollary 2.5]).}
elements.sent-2411-186: Because $\Po$ has no chain containing more than 3 elements, the non-extremal intervals are pairwise intersecting.
elements.sent-2411-187: Therefore, by Helly's theorem \cite{Helly , non-extremal intervals contain a common line $\ell_0$.}
elements.sent-2411-188: All those intervals containing $\ell_0$ form the {\it middle part .}
elements.sent-2411-189: Note that the middle part may contain extremal intervals (see an example in Figure \ref{abra9 ).}
elements.sent-2411-190: The second stage produces a representation of $P$ that has depth-2 such that intervals in the middle part have length $\alpha$ or $\beta$, and the length of further extremal intervals is less than $\alpha$.
elements.sent-2411-191: In the third stage the endpoints of these `short' extremal intervals are moved to obtain each length equal to $\alpha$.. 1.
elements.sent-2411-192: We may assume that $P$ has no twins\footnote{\ Elements of a poset having the same up-sets and the same down-sets are called twins.. .}
elements.sent-2411-193: Due to this assumption, given two intervals, either their right endpoints or their left endpoints are distinct.
elements.sent-2411-194: If there is a non-extremal singleton, then it is the only one at $\ell_0$.
elements.sent-2411-195: In this case, we replace the singleton with an interval $I_0$ of length $\epsilon>0$ around $\ell_0$ in every interval containing $\ell_0$.
elements.sent-2411-196: Intervals with common endpoints are changed by shifting some endpoints into newly introduced lines holding the separated endpoints as follows.
elements.sent-2411-197: Left endpoints of the intervals at each line are shifted to left, right endpoints to right by eliminating inclusions.
elements.sent-2411-198: For each $i$, $0< i<\ell_0$, there is a set $E$ of intervals with left endpoint at line $i$.
elements.sent-2411-199: We distinguish the left endpoints of all these intervals in the same order as their right endpoints (see Figure \ref{abra12 ).}
elements.sent-2411-200: Because non-extremal intervals contain $\ell_0$, their right endpoints are greater than $\ell_0$.
elements.sent-2411-201: Therefore, the extremal interval $I_e=[0,i]$ belongs to $\mathcal C$.
elements.sent-2411-202: We keep the right endpoint of $I_e$ at line $i$ together with the left endpoint of the largest interval in $E$, all other left endpoints at $i$ move left into distinct new lines subdividing the gap $[i-1,i]$.
elements.sent-2411-203: The intervals modified in this way still represent $\Po$.
elements.sent-2411-204: In the example of the figure above tie breaking is applied with $E=\{x,y,z\}$ leading to \ $i-1< \ell_y < \ell_z < \ell_x=r_{e =i$.}
elements.sent-2411-205: By switching to the dual of $\Po$, the same shifting is applied for the coinciding right endpoints in each line $j$ ($m-1>j>\ell_0$).. 2.
elements.sent-2411-206: Let $I_j=[\ell_j,r_j]_{j=1 ^k$, be the intervals (extremal, non-extremal), containing $\ell_0$.}
elements.sent-2411-207: We may assume \begin{equation}\ell_{\pi(1)}<\ell_{\pi(2)}<\cdots<\ell_{\pi(k)} < \ell_0 < r_1<r_2<\cdots<r_k,\end{equation} where $\pi$ is the permutation of $\{1,2,\ldots,k\}$, defined by the ordering of the left endpoints in the representation obtained in stage 2.
elements.sent-2411-208: Due to the tie breaking rule applied in stage 1, and because $\Po$ has depth 2, there is no nested chain of more than two intervals; in particular, $\pi$ is a depth-2 permutation.
elements.sent-2411-209: Applying Theorem \ref{2countperm , we obtain an interval representation $\mathcal R$ of $\pi$ using interval lengths $\alpha < \beta$.}
elements.sent-2411-210: We select the unit in the 2-count representation large enough so that $\alpha$ is larger than all interval lengths of extremal intervals not in the middle part.. 3.
elements.sent-2411-211: Merging the middle part with extremal intervals.
elements.sent-2411-212: Let O and M be the first and last line of the 2-count representation $\mathcal R$ obtained for the intervals of $P$ in the middle part.
elements.sent-2411-213: Now we include all extremal intervals not present in $\mathcal R$ (see the example below).
elements.sent-2411-214: Because there is no containment between extremal intervals, after breaking the ties, each of these extremal intervals can be adjusted to have their length equal to $\alpha$ by extending them to left, whenever they used to contain line $0$ in $\mathcal C$, and by extending to right, whenever they used to contain line $m-1$ in $\mathcal C$.
elements.sent-2411-215: We conclude the section with an example.
elements.sent-2411-216: Consider the depth-2, height-3 interval order $P=(0,1,0,1,2,1,0,2,0,2,0,3)$ identified here using its Ascent Sequence.
elements.sent-2411-217: The procedure in the proof of Theorem \ref{2count starts with the canonical representation of $P$ and yields the middle part permutation $\pi=\{1,3,4,5,2\}$ after breaking ties.}
elements.sent-2411-218: Then using the sorted 2-coloring $S=\{1,2\}$, $T=\{3,4,5\}$ Theorem \ref{2countperm finds a solution with $\alpha=9, \beta= 13$ for the middle part.}
elements.sent-2411-219: Theorem \ref{2count completes the 2-count representation of $P$ by merging the middle part with the extremal intervals $6,7,8$ and $9,10,11,12$.}
elements.sent-2411-220: In the present section $\pi$ is a depth-3 permutation of $[n]$; let $\{I_j\}_{j\in[n]}$ be an interval representation of $\pi$.
elements.sent-2411-221: Recall that a sorted $3$-coloring $[T_1, T_2, T_3]$ of $\pi$ satisfies that $T_i$ contains no inversion, for $i=1,2,3$, and if $x\in T_i$, $y\in T_j$ and $I_x\subsetneq I_y$, then $i<j$.
elements.sent-2411-222: A sorted $3$-coloring $[T_1, T_2, T_3]$ is {\it feasible , provided there exist integers $\alpha_1<\alpha_2<\alpha_3$, and an integral representation $[\ell_x,r_x]_{x=1 ^n$ such that $r_x-\ell_x=\alpha_i$ for every $x\in T_i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-223: There are several examples of depth-3 permutations with infeasible sorted 3-colorings.
elements.sent-2411-224: This does not mean that the underlying permutation has no other feasible sorted $3$-coloring.
elements.sent-2411-225: However, any such permutation can be embedded into a larger depth-3 permutation such that this infeasible coloring is forced by all sorted 3-colorings of the larger permutation.
elements.sent-2411-226: Computer search shows that the smallest depth-3 permutation with no 3-count representation is $\pi=[4,2,5,10,3,1,7,6,9,8]$ shown earlier in Figure \ref{abra6 .}
elements.sent-2411-227: We prove next that this permutation has no 3-count representation.
elements.sent-2411-229: The inclusion poset of $\{I_x\}_{x\in[10]}$ in Figure \ref{abra7 shows that $5,10\in T_3, 2,9\in T_2$ and $1,6\in T_1$ in every sorted $3$-coloring $[T_1,T_2,T_3]$ of $\pi$.}
elements.sent-2411-230: This means that when $\pi$ is restricted to the permutation $\pi^\prime=[2,5,10,1,6,9]$ on the subset $\{1,2,5,6,9,10\}$, the induced sorted $3$-coloring is $T_1^\prime= \{1,6\}, \quad T_2^\prime=\{2,9\}, \quad T_3^\prime=\{5,10\}$.
elements.sent-2411-231: If this sorted $3$-coloring is feasible, then there is a representation $[\ell_i,r_i]$, $i\in\{1,2,5,6,9,10\}$ of $\pi$ such that \begin{eqnarray}\label{x}\ell_6-\ell_1 = r_6-r_1 \label{y}\ell_9-\ell_2 = r_9-r_2 \label{z}\ell_{10}-\ell_5 = r_{10}-r_5\end{eqnarray}.
elements.sent-2411-234: Therefore, $\pi^\prime$ and thus $\pi$ has no 3-count representation.
elements.sent-2411-235: In this section, we add a few comments and highlight significant open problems.
elements.sent-2411-236: It is worth repeating that Graham's original $k$-count problem on interval orders remains open for $k \geq 2$.
elements.sent-2411-237: In particular, characterizing $2$-count interval orders remains unsolved despite having received attention for special cases \cite{BoyadzhiyskaDissertation,BGT,FMOS,JLORS .}
elements.sent-2411-238: Adding another interesting special case, we conjecture that a $\{\text{spring, \mathbf{4 +\mathbf{1}\}$-free interval order is $2$-count.}}
elements.sent-2411-239: During his investigations of $2$-count interval orders, Fishburn proved \cite[Section 9, Theorem 1]{FishburnBook that for arbitrary $k$, there are depth-$2$ interval orders with no $k$-count interval representation.}
elements.sent-2411-240: His recursive construction includes an {$\mathbf{r +\mathbf{1}$} subposet (an $r$-element chain with an additional incomparable element) for increasingly larger values of $r$.}
elements.sent-2411-241: For fixed $r$, does an upper bound on the count number of an {$\mathbf{r +\mathbf{1}$}-free interval order follow from a bound on its depth? For example, is a depth-$2$, {$\mathbf{4 +\mathbf{1}$}-free interval order necessarily $5$-count? We showed (Theorem \ref{2countperm ) that depth-$2$ permutations have a $2$-count representation.}}}
elements.sent-2411-242: Meanwhile, we found several depth-$3$ permutations that are not $3$-count.
elements.sent-2411-243: Can one describe all depth-$3$ permutations that are minimally not $3$-count? Is there a bound on the count number of a permutation in terms of its depth? For example, does a depth-$3$ permutation necessarily have a $5$-count interval representation? \bibliographystyle{amsplain.}
